label: ai
topic: A father with Parkinson's collapses the night of a rocket launch
text:
The launch is scheduled for 9:47 p.m., and your father wants to watch from the porch. He has been talking about it all week — a cargo resupply mission, not the most glamorous kind but the most beautiful, he says, because the rocket is lighter without a crew and climbs faster, a bright needle threading the dark.

You help him into the chair. This is a production now. The Parkinson's has progressed to where sitting is a negotiation between his body and gravity, his hand on your forearm, his weight shifting in increments. Once seated, he exhales as if he has accomplished something significant. He has.

Three weeks since his third spinal surgery. His spine is a cathedral of titanium hardware, and still his hands shake when he lifts a glass, still his feet shuffle on the linoleum.

The porch faces east. You can see the causeway, twenty miles across the water to the launch pad. The air smells like salt and jasmine from the bush your mother planted the year of the diagnosis, the year everything became before and after.

"Tell me about the rocket," you say. You do not want to talk about the appointment Thursday or the physical therapist who leaves looking discouraged.

"Falcon 9. Reusable first stage. The booster lands on a drone ship in the Atlantic." He is animated now, his voice stronger than it has been all day. This is what space does for him. It lifts him out of the body that is failing, into a place where physics is elegant and things work the way they should.

He tells you about orbital mechanics, about the Tsiolkovsky equation. You understand a third of it. What matters is how he leans forward, the tremor still there but secondary beneath his enthusiasm.

"Two minutes," he says.

You think about your father in the hospital three weeks ago, his back opened for the third time, and how the body is a kind of spacecraft too — a pressurised vessel carrying a person through an environment trying to kill them.

A glow appears on the horizon. Then the rocket, a column of white fire rising from the tree line. The sound arrives seconds later, a rumble you feel in your sternum. Your father grips the chair arm, his face lit by distant fire.

"There it goes," he says.

It arcs eastward, the flame shrinking. A controlled explosion carrying supplies to six people two hundred and fifty miles above you.

Your father stands without asking for help, which is how you know something is wrong. He takes one step and his legs fold, not dramatically but like a slow demolition. He goes to his knees, then his side, and you are there, hands on his shoulders, saying his name.

"I'm all right," he says. He is not. His face is grey.

You call your mother. You call the ambulance. You sit on the porch with his head in your lap and the rocket's trail fading above, a white line already dissolving.

At the hospital, they adjust medications. Your father lies in a bed too short for him and asks if the booster landed.

You look it up. It did. On a drone ship called Of Course I Still Love You, a name from a science fiction novel.

"It landed," you tell him.

He closes his eyes and smiles. Outside, the sky is full of objects in motion — satellites, stations, debris, all falling and missing the ground, which is all that orbit is. You hold his hand and think about return journeys, about the booster falling back through the atmosphere, finding the ship. About how the hardest part of any mission is not the launch. It is coming home.
